title: From conflict to repair
description: Fighting fair, and fixing it after you mess up.
summaryPoints: Aim for the fix, not the win — it's the two of you vs the problem
Stay on ONE issue and never go for contempt or name-calling
When you're flooded, take a real timeout before you say more
Give a real apology: name it, own it with no 'but', say what changes
Repair is the skill — small reconnects matter more than never fighting

content.title: Conflict isn't the enemy
content.body:
Every close relationship has fights. That's not a glitch — it's two people who care enough to disagree.

Handled well, a conflict can actually make you closer. Avoided or fought dirty, it slowly corrodes the whole thing.

So the skill was never "never fight." The skill is fighting in a way that leaves the relationship standing — and fixing it fast when you slip.

content.title: The fix, not the win
content.body:
When a fight starts, your brain wants to **win**. Score the point. Prove you're right.

But winning an argument with someone you love is how you lose, slowly. The reframe that changes everything:

It's not **you vs them**. It's the **two of you vs the problem**.

Same team, facing the thing together. From there, you're solving — not battling.
content.imageAlt: A cracked bridge being mended, standing for repair after conflict

content.prompt: Winning the argument is the goal of a healthy conflict.
content.explanation: The goal is the fix, not the win — it's the two of you against the problem, not against each other.

content.context: Your roommate left dishes again and you're annoyed.
options: "You're so lazy, you never clean up."
"I'm frustrated about the dishes piling up — can we sort a system?"
"Fine, I'll just do everything myself like always."
"Why are you incapable of basic adulting?"
content.explanation: Naming your feeling and the problem keeps you on the same team; the others attack the person and invite a fight.

content.title: Fight fair: the rules
content.body:
Fighting fair has a few simple rules that keep a disagreement from becoming a war:
- Use **I-statements** — own your feeling instead of accusing
- Stay on **ONE issue** — don't drag in every past grievance
- **No jugular** — no name-calling, no "you always," no contempt

Contempt — eye-rolls, mockery, "you're pathetic" — is the single most damaging move in any relationship. It's the one to cut first.

items.text: "I felt hurt when you canceled last minute"
Rolling your eyes and calling them pathetic
Sticking to the one thing that's bothering you
"And ANOTHER thing from three months ago…"
Taking a breath before you respond

content.instructions: One word makes this advice backwards. Tap it.
content.sentence: A fair fight stays on every issue at once.
content.explanation: Fight fair by sticking to one issue — dragging in every past grievance guarantees nothing gets resolved.

content.title: When you're flooded
content.body:
Sometimes mid-argument your heart pounds, your face gets hot, and you genuinely **can't think straight**. That's called being flooded — your body has hit fight-or-flight.

Nothing useful gets said while you're flooded. You'll just hurt each other.

The move: call a real timeout. "I need twenty minutes, then let's finish this." Cool down — and actually come back. A timeout isn't running away.

content.prompt: You're flooded mid-argument — heart pounding, can't think. Best move?
options: Push through and say whatever comes out
Storm off and never bring it up again
Ask for a short timeout, then come back to finish it
Win fast before you lose your nerve
content.explanation: A real timeout lets your body settle so you can talk instead of wound — but you have to actually return.

content.title: A real apology
content.body:
Everyone messes up. What separates people isn't whether they rupture — it's whether they **repair**.

A real apology has four parts:
- **Name it** — "I snapped at you"
- **Own it** — no "but," no excuse
- **Acknowledge the impact** — "that wasn't fair, and it hurt you"
- **Say what changes** — "I'll take a breath next time"

"I'm sorry you feel that way" is NOT an apology — it blames their feelings instead of owning what you did.

content.prompt: "I'm sorry you feel that way" counts as a real apology.
content.explanation: That phrase blames their feelings and owns nothing — a real apology names what YOU did.

content.context: You blew up at a friend and want to make it right.
options: "I'm sorry you got so upset about it."
"I'm sorry, but you kind of started it."
"I snapped at you and that wasn't fair — I'll cool down before I talk next time."
"My bad, can we just move on?"
content.explanation: It names the behavior, owns it with no 'but,' acknowledges the impact, and commits to a change — all four parts.

content.prompt: Match each defensive habit to the repair move that beats it.

content.title: Repair is the whole game
content.body:
Healthy relationships aren't the ones that never rupture. They're the ones that **repair quickly**.

A repair can be tiny: a joke, a hand on a shoulder, "can we start over?" These little reaches are what keep things from hardening.

And sometimes the wisest repair is to just let a small thing go. Not every annoyance needs a summit.
content.imageAlt: Two halves of a bridge reconnecting after a crack

content.prompt: Put a clean repair in order, from the heat of the moment to fixed.
steps: Cool down until you can think clearly
Name the impact it had on them
Make a real apology and a small reach-back
content.explanation: Settle first, then own it, then show you see the impact — a rushed apology while flooded usually misfires.

content.prompt: Think of a recent friction with someone you care about. What's one small repair you could make this week?
content.context: No big summit required — just one honest reach.
content.explanation: Repair is a muscle. The smallest honest reach keeps a relationship from hardening.

content.title: The repair
content.intro: You just snapped at someone you care about. Trust took a hit. Walk it back.

steps.situation: Right after you snap, your heart's pounding and you're still hot. What now?

options.text: Take a breath and cool down first
options.feedback: Smart — nothing good gets said while you're flooded.

options.text: Keep going while you've got momentum
options.feedback: Flooded means you'll wound, not solve.
Defending skips straight past the repair.

steps.situation: You're calmer. You open your mouth to address it.

options.feedback: That blames their feelings and owns nothing.

options.text: "I snapped at you, and that was unfair."
options.feedback: Naming exactly what you did is the heart of a real apology.

options.text: "Well, you started it last week too."
options.feedback: Dragging up old mistakes blows up the repair.

steps.situation: They look hurt. You want them to know you see it.

options.text: "It wasn't that big a deal, honestly."
options.feedback: Minimizing their hurt reopens the wound.

options.text: Say nothing and hope it blows over
options.feedback: Silence reads as stonewalling, not repair.

options.text: "That wasn't fair to you, and I get why it stung."
options.feedback: Acknowledging the impact shows you actually see them.

steps.situation: Time to close the loop and reconnect.

options.text: "I'll take a breath next time. Can we start over?"
options.feedback: A change plus a small reach-back is exactly how repair lands.
Rushing past it leaves the rupture unhealed.

options.text: "Just don't make me that mad again."
options.feedback: Putting it back on them undoes the whole apology.

content.outro: Cool down, own it, name the impact, reach back. That's repair — and it's the whole skill.
